observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for transfers of agency’s unutilized DRRMF to the special trust fund by year of transfer, receipts of NDRRMF by transferring agency, DRRMF from other LGUs by LGU and other source by donor recorded under Trust Liability-DRRM, if any; and
Ensure that the amount and details of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF of the Municipality per year be reflected in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Management:
Adhere to the provision of Section 5 DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 to ensure the optimal utilization of 20% Development Fund to help achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the Municipality;
Submit justifications for the delay and non-implementation/completion of the above-mentioned priority projects aggregating P64.407 million from 
CYs 2020-2023; and
Initiate corrective measures to fasttrack the implementation and completion of unimplemented and ongoing priority projects for CYs 2020-2023 in order to avoid accumulation of unimplemented projects and to ensure the optimal utilization of 20% Development Fund in the ensuing year at lower cost due to soaring prices of commodities.

We recommended that the Management, through the Human Resource Management Office, require the execution of written contracts of service prior to the hiring of job orders to legally bind the parties; and secure prior authorization from the Sanggunian before entering into such contracts, in compliance with Section 2 of CSC Resolution No. 020790 dated June 5, 2002 and Section 22 (c) of the Local Government Code, as previously recommended, to avoid suspensions and/or disallowances in audit. Further, we recommended that the Management submit proof of the reassessment of manpower complement and the lack of funds for the additional Plantilla position.

We required that the Management submit their justification on their practice of issuing the Certificate of Completion and Certificate of Acceptance simultaneously with the Statement of Work Accomplished, which is non-compliant with Section 62.2.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of Republic Act No. 9184. We also recommended that the Management stop such practice in order to secure the warranty period prescribed in the said provision.

We recommended that the Management strictly adhere with the provisions of
Item 4 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 and Item 3.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-01 dated June 14, 2012 on the submission, and liquidation of fund transfers received from National Government Agencies (NGAs). 

Further, we recommended that the Management require the Municipal Accountant to immediately collate records to ensure that all unspent balances of fund transfers for completed projects, irrespective of source agency, be returned, and the concerned Departments in charge, ensure that the programs and projects are promptly implemented to maximize the benefits that could be derived therefrom.

We recommended that the Management instruct the LDRRMO to prepare annually the LDRRMFIP duly approved by the LCE using the sample format in Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, and include under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years, pursuant to Item 5.1.2 of the same Circular.

We required that the Management submit their justification on the award of the contract despite the discrepancy in the program of works and bill of quantities by the contractor. We also recommended that the Management strictly comply with Sections 31 and 32 of the 2016 Revised Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184 for the award of future contracts for infrastructure projects.

We recommended that the Management submit the documentary requirements to support the disbursement for the Collective Negotiation Agreement granted to the LGU officials and employees amounting to P4,432,530.00 to facilitate verification of the legality and propriety of the transaction and to avoid suspensions in audit.

We recommended that the Management, thru the Local Chief Executive, undertake the following courses of actions:

1.	Collect the liquidated damages aggregating P637,816.40 from the suppliers who incurred delays in their deliveries, otherwise, they will be held responsible for the amount.

2.	Ensure that the terms and conditions indicated in the purchase orders and contracts are strictly complied with.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Effect the refund of cash advance for travelling expenses through salary deductions as provided under Item 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002, where necessary, to avoid sanctions provided by law; and
Require the other concerned employees to liquidate immediately all their outstanding cash advances. Refrain from granting additional cash advances to these employees who still have unliquidated cash advances and observe strictly the rules and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that the Management require the Municipal Treasurer to submit the cancelled checks to the Municipal Accountant and for the latter to submit immediately all the supporting documents of the BRS for the year 2023, such as original copies of the bank statements, debit and credit memos, cancelled and paid checks and all supporting documents necessary to facilitate the timely verification of Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements submitted to the Office of the Auditor, and submit all succeeding monthly BRS with complete supporting documents pursuant to Paragraph 3.0 of COA Circular No. 96-011, dated October 2, 1996.

We recommended that the Management submit immediately the perfected contracts, together with the supporting documents, for CY 2023 to afford the Audit Team a prompt review and evaluation thereof. Moreover, the Management should strictly comply with the submission/transmittal of executed contracts together with the supporting documents within five (5) days after its perfection to avoid suspensions in audit and impose sanctions to erring officials for non-compliance thereof, if necessary.

We required that the Management submit their justification on the inclusion of “quality control” work item in their program of work and bill of quantities, which added to the cost of the project and stop such practice in order to comply with the provision of Section 5(u) of the 2016 Revised Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184, otherwise, any future inclusion of such work item will be disallowed in audit.

We required that the Management submit their justification on their reference to the preferred brand of the vehicles purchased and stop indicating a particular brand in the Purchase Request, but only include the relevant characteristics and performance requirements of the items to be procured, otherwise, future references will be acted severely. Lastly, we recommended that brand names, together with the specifications of the items quoted by the supplier in response to the advertised relevant characteristics and performance requirement of the items needed, be included in the Purchase Order to serve as reference during deliveries and inspection and acceptance by the Management.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee submit immediately the financial components of the winning bidder for our review and evaluation and scrutinize all details of the forms embodied in the contract documents for proper and valid reporting of contract documents, in compliance to the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:
Exert more effort in tracing the acquisition cost of the properties presented in the RCPPE and update the Report on the Physical Count of Road Network System in order to establish the cost, existence, ownership and control of those properties;
Undertake all the procedures prescribed under COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to come up with the reconciled and correct balances of Property, Plant and Equipment accounts in the financial statements; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to record the necessary adjustments in order to present a fair and reliable balance of the Property, Plant and Equipment in accordance with PPSAS 17.

We recommended that the Management undertake the necessary procedures and validate the existence of the receivables for the purpose of filing a written request for authority to write-off the accounts, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Management fast-track the implementation of the current year and continuing development projects, in order to avoid accumulation of unimplemented projects, and to ensure the optimal utilization of the 20% development fund in the ensuing year, pursuant to Section 5 of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017, for the benefit of the constituents of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipality, through the Human Resource Management Office, require the execution of written contract of service prior to the hiring of job orders to legally bind the parties; and secure prior authorization from the Sanggunian before entering into such contract, in compliance to Section 2 of CSC Resolution No. 020790 dated June 5, 2002 and Section 22 (c) of the Local Government Code.
We also recommended that the Management re-assess their manpower complement and propose to the Sanggunian the creation and funding of additional plantillas, as the budgetary limit for personnel services may allow, in order to reduce the hiring of unlimited job orders, so as not to prejudice good governance, fiscal adequacy and public service delivery.

We recommended that the Management include in the LDRRMF Investment Plan or revised Annual Investment Plan (AIP) the projects/activities/programs chargeable against the “Trust Liabilities- DRRMF” account of prior years’ unexpended balance, pursuant to NDRRMC-DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 and strictly comply with the accounting guidelines and disclosures of LDRRMF under COA Circular No. 2012-002. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain Subsidiary Ledgers (SL) for the yearly transfer of unspent LDRRMF to the Special Trust Fund and provide disclosures thereof in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Management align the projects to be funded in the 20% Development Fund with the allowed priority projects, in compliance with the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017, in order to contribute to the socio-economic development and environmental management of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Management immediately prepare the corresponding liquidation documents to be submitted to the Source Agency, and return the unutilized balances of the fund in accordance with Item 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994, thus, reducing the liability of the LGU. We also recommended that the Management timely implement the funded programs or projects by the National Government in order to maximize the use of government resources and prompt delivery of the desired socio- economic outcomes to its constituents.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee fully comply with the requirements of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184 in the preparation and evaluation of bidding documents to foster attainment of the principles of transparency, competitiveness and system of accountability on government procurement.

We recommended that the Management submit justification as to why the total amount of P5,854,852.22 should not be disallowed in audit. Further, we recommended that the Management utilize solely the LDRRM Fund to implement projects and activities under the legislated thematic areas, namely: 1) disaster prevention and mitigation; 2) disaster preparedness; 3) disaster response; and 4) disaster rehabilitation and recovery, as provided in Section 21 of RA 10121 and Section 5.0 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

We recommended that the Management immediately require the refund by all payees of the entire amount of P3,060,000.00 paid out of the municipal funds for this Calamity Assistance, otherwise, the same shall be disallowed in audit. We also recommended that the Management pay only those allowances and benefits that are expressly provided in Republic Act No. 6758 and COA Circular 2013-003 dated January 30, 2013.

We recommended that the Management:
Stop the practice of granting of cash advances for allowable purposes without the supporting documentary requirements enumerated in COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14,2012;
Ensure that cash advances are only granted to authorized or designated disbursing officers and are sufficiently bonded as required under Item 3.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10,1997; and
Submit immediately all the liquidation reports to the Audit Team for further verification and review; and monitor liquidation of subsequent cash advances in compliance to Section 339 of the Republic Act No. 7160 and Section 89 of the Presidential Decree No. 1445.

We recommended that the Management properly plan and establish a well-defined policy on the procurement of meals and snacks that will be served during the programmed/planned activities for the year in order to ensure wide dissemination of bid opportunities and obtain transparency on the conduct of public bidding, in compliance to the provisions of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184.

We recommended that:
the Municipal Accountant/Barangay Bookkeeper require the Barangay Treasurers to submit immediately the copy of their accounts to the Accounting Office to facilitate the preparation and submission of the required reports and promptly submit the complete set of Financial Statements of the Barangay, in compliance with Section 10.4 of the Manual on the Financial Management of Barangays;
the Municipal Mayor consider assigning additional personnel to assist in bookkeeping barangay accounts; and
the Municipal Mayor consider suspension of payment of the salaries of the officials concerned until they shall have complied with the requirements of the Commission.

We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit immediately to the Audit Team all previously cancelled checks for audit inspection and verification and thereafter attach all cancelled checks to the Report of Checks Issued (RCI) for timely validation of the status of these checks, pursuant to Section 59 of the Local Treasury Operations Manual.

We recommended that the Management submit immediately to the Audit Team the contracts, together with the supporting documents, for CY 2022 to facilitate early review and evaluation process by the Auditor concerned, especially those projects that were already completed and paid, otherwise, these contracts will be suspended in audit. Further, we recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee exert effort in ensuring that copies of future contract documents will be submitted within five (5) days from execution of the project in compliance with Item 3.1.1 COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We required that the Management submit their justification on their reference to brand names in their purchase of medical equipment.
We also recommended that the Management stop indicating a particular brand in the Purchase Request, but only include the relevant characteristics and performance requirements of the items to be procured. Lastly, we recommended that brand names, together with the specifications of the items quoted by the supplier in response to the advertised relevant characteristics and performance requirement of the items needed, be included in the Purchase Order to serve as reference during deliveries and inspection and acceptance by the Management.

We recommended that the Management submit the duly accomplished Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation for CY 2022 by requiring the Department Heads/employees concerned to communicate the action/s undertaken and proposed action plans on the audit findings and recommendations for the year 2022 including that of prior years, within sixty (60) days from their receipt of the COA Annual Audit Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned Department Heads to undertake their responsibilities provided in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 to restate the “Road Network” account to its appropriate and fair balance.

We recommended, pursuant to Sections 182, 187, 189 and 257 of the Volume 1 of the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs to set up the proper safeguards on various supplies, drugs and medicines that:
1. The GSO to accept the deliveries from suppliers and issue Requisition and Issue Slip for its transfer to the end-user. He should also maintain stock cards for each inventory item and submit to the Municipal Accountant the required Report of Materials and Supplies Issued five days after the end of the month;
2. The Municipal Accountant record all inventories as asset upon delivery and acceptance and maintain Supplies Ledger Card for each specific type of Inventories; and
3. The Municipal Mayor require periodic physical inventory of supplies and materials. The physical count of unissued inventory items by type shall be conducted at least once a year and reported in the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI).

We recommended that Management advise the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to trace the details of the account no. 0505-03927T-531 with year-end balance of P339,024.72 as confirmed by the bank and immediately record the same in the books of the LGU pursuant to Section 74 of PD 1445 to avoid misuse and misappropriation of the fund and to reflect the correct balance of the “Cash in Bank” account in the financial statements.

We recommended the immediate refund by all payees of the entire amount of P5,640,000.00 paid out of the municipal funds for this “Economic Recovery Aid” for calendar year 2020. We also recommended that Management pay only those allowances and benefits that are expressly provided in RA 6758 and COA Circular No. 2013-003 dated January 30, 2013 to avoid disallowances in audit.

We recommended that the Management demand the liquidation of the financial assistance and to fully adhere to the guidelines and documentary requirements provided by COA Circular No. 2012-001 for optimal utilization of funds and that expenditures charged therefrom are aligned with the purpose indicated in the memorandum of agreement.

We recommended management to post all the required information in the GPPB Online Portal relating to emergency procurement and comply with all other requirements to ensure transparency and accountability in the implementation of the Bayanihan Act in government procurement as mandated under Republic Act No. 11469.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the rules and regulations on the creation of special accounts in the General Fund and direct the Accountant to maintain special account for the BGCM and make necessary adjustments on the accounts affected for fair presentation in the books of the municipality in accord with Local Budget Circular No. 125.

We recommended that Management submit a justification on why the subject transactions should be allowed in audit. We likewise recommended that Management refrain from purchasing unnecessarily high-specification ICT equipment and ensure moderate and economical government transactions to avoid extravagant expenditures not allowed by COA Circular No. 2012-003.

We recommended that Management stop paying for the meals and snacks served during meetings, conferences and sessions of the Sangguniang Bayan of the municipality in accord with Section 343 of R.A. 7160.

We recommended that Management grant/draw cash advances based on approved payrolls and other necessary supporting documents in accordance with COA Circular No. 1997-002 dated February 10, 1997. Payrolls should be timely prepared by the HRMO to facilitate the grant and release of cash advance. The LGU should implement control measures to ascertain prompt liquidation of cash advances to safeguard government funds and to forestall abuse and the incurrence of losses due to mishandling and misappropriation.
We also recommended that Management disburse payrolls to the bank accounts of the job order/contractual employees, to the greatest extent possible, through the automatic debit advice (ADA) facility of the Municipality’s depository bank/s, thus, decrease the need for payroll cash advances. Likewise, this disbursement system would forestall personal contact with and among the various payees, pursuant to the health and safety protocols required under Republic Act No. 11469 dated March 20, 2020, thereby, managing the risks involved with the COVID-19 pandemic.

We recommended that the management grant cash advance only to employees duly appointed/designated as Disbursing Officer in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 and comply with the bonding requirement as prescribed therein, to ensure that government funds are safeguarded from the risk of possible loss arising from theft, misuse or misappropriation.

We recommended that the Management submit an explanation/justification why the subject transactions should be allowed in audit. We further reminded that Management acquire vehicles which are practical and economical, in accordance with the pertinent provisions of DBM Budget Circular No. 2017-1 dated April 26, 2017 and Administrative Order No. 14 dated December 10, 2018, to ensure efficiency, economy and prudence in public spending.

We recommended that Management instruct the BAC to:
1. Upload the Bidding Documents in the PhilGEPS pursuant to Section 17.4 of the Revised Implement Rules and Regulations (RIRR) of Republic Act (RA) No. 9184 to give interested bidders sufficient information about the procurement at hand;
2. Send invitations to representatives from a) duly recognized private group and b) non-government organization (NGO) to attend as observers in the procurement proceedings in compliance with Section 13 of the said RIRR; and
3. Furnish the Audit Team copies of approved Bidding Documents in accordance with Section 17 of the RIRR.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the provisions of RA 9679 in observing the maximum limitation of government share of the employees’ PAG-IBIG contributions at P100.00 per month per official/employee.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer to withhold the prescribed retention money from payments of contract billings pursuant to Item 6 of Annex E of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184 to cover project’s defects and third-party liabilities that may arise before final acceptance.

We recommended that management:
1. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to immediately prepare a disbursement voucher to effect the transfer from the Trust Fund to the General Fund in order to present the correct balance of the “Cash in Bank” account in the financial statements of both funds of the Municipality pursuant to Section 305 of the Local Government Code and Section 4(3) of PD 1445; and
2. Regularly monitor intra-agency accounts and immediately effect transfers to/from other funds to avoid cash overdrafts.

We recommended that Management:
1. Instruct the Accounting Office and the BAC to submit to the Audit Team the Omnibus Sworn Statement, certificate of PhilGEPS registration and other lacking required eligibility documents pursuant to Section 54.6 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184 and Section 4 of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020, to avoid suspension and disallowance in audit; and
2. Comply strictly to the prescribed documentary requirements required by the RIRR of RA 9184 and other GPPB issuances in all procurements undertaken by the LGU.

We recommended that Management submit the duly notarized SPA issued by concerned supplier and instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to ascertain that payments shall be exclusively made and released to duly authorized payees to avoid invalid payments and irregular disbursement of government funds, thereby, ensure compliance with the complete documentation requirements provided in Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445.

We recommended that the management secure Certificate of Availability of Funds for appropriation duly signed by the Municipal Accountant as prescribed under the Budget Operations Manual for Local Government Units 2016 Edition and Local Budget Circular No. 112 dated June 10, 2016 to ensure that all appropriations are supported by existing cash back up.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive increase the fidelity bond coverage of the Disbursing Officers based on their actual maximum accountability to appropriately insure the municipal funds against possible losses arising from defalcation, shortages and unrelieved accountability on account of the bonded officers pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 dated August 6, 2009.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and other concerned Officers to promptly submit the payrolls, disbursement vouchers and the supporting documents to the Audit Team within ten (10) days from receipt of the documents pursuant to Section 6.05 of COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995, otherwise, the same shall be considered non-rendition of accounts which is punishable by law.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct all the Department Heads/employees concerned to submit their action/s undertaken and proposed action plans on the audit findings and recommendations for the year 2019 including that of prior years, related/connected to their departments/offices and the information gathered therein be used to properly accomplish the AAPSI for submission to the COA Auditor within five (5) days from receipt of this AOM.
We further recommended that henceforth, the Management submit regularly to the COA, either in printed form or by way of duly approved electronic document, a status report on the actions taken on said audit findings and recommendations within sixty (60) days from their receipt of the COA Annual Audit Report.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Trace the period or date of acquisition of each item of PPE and its components and compute their respective depreciation based on the estimated useful life of assets determined appropriate by management and book-up prior years’ deprecation as prior year adjustment to be closed to the Government Equity Account; and
b. Provide for yearly depreciation of the abovementioned assets from 2019 onwards pursuant to Paragraph 4 and 6 of PPSAS 17.

The Legislative Body of the Municipality formulate a policy ordinance to govern the minimum qualification standards and the financial parameters for the maximum limit of job order/contract of service workers that may be hired for specific non-regular functions and tasks towards the efficient, effective and economical implementation of programs, projects and activities of the LGU and operations of economic enterprises.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the policies regarding the withholding of retention money to cover the defects that may arise before final acceptance of the project, pursuant to Item 6 of Annex E of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184.

We recommended that Management:
Stop the practice of acquiring goods out of cash advances and ensure that all cash advances granted are within the allowable purpose as prescribed under COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997; and
Ensure that all the procurement transactions and activities of the Municipality are in accordance with the prescribed procedures as provided in the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184, otherwise known as the Government Procurement Reform Act.

We recommended that Management include in the LDRRMF Investment Plan or revised Annual Investment Plan (AIP) the projects/activities/programs chargeable against the “Trust Liabilities- DRRMF” account of prior years pursuant to NDRRMC-DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 and the same be formulated in adherence to the format prescribed in Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 for proper accounting and reporting of the fund.

We recommended that Management ensure the immediate implementation and completion of ongoing and unimplemented projects, in order to effectively increase the level of preparedness of the community from the occurrence of natural calamities and mitigate the risk of damage in case of disaster.

We recommended that the Municipal Development Council thoroughly and meticulously plan and review the projects, programs and activities included in the Municipality’s AIP and ensure that projects to be funded out of the 20% development fund are purely capital expenditure in accordance with the provisions of DILG DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017- 1,  to contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management goal of the National Government as indicated in the Philippine Development Plan and Public Investment Program.

We recommended that Management revive the Community Seed Banking Program or propose to the Department of Agriculture the use of the fund for other related programs to efficiently utilize the balance of the “Community Seed Banking Fund” for the benefit of the farmers, otherwise, return the same pursuant to the provisions of Section 4 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 and Section 3.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that Management:
Institute proper and sound internal control on the grant and utilization of cash advances (CAs), pursuant to Sections 123 and 124 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 by refraining the grant of CAs intended for multiple recipients to a single accountable officer (AO) to avoid its irregular transfer from one AO to another as prohibited in Section 4.1.6 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and in order to enable the Municipality to establish their respective individual accountabilities and enforce subsequent liquidation by the employees/officials who were the actual recipients of the cash advance;
Monitor the propriety and reasonableness of all claims for cash advances to ensure that it shall be granted to allowed officials and employees only at the appropriate and reasonable amount in compliance with the procedural rules and guidelines provided in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997; and
Instruct the concerned employee to refund immediately the double payment for accommodation and transportation expenses amounting to P30,105.00 claimed for the travel to Metro Manila on January 28 to February 2, 2019 under Travel Order No. 2019-076, otherwise, a Notice of Disallowance shall be issued in accordance with COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the erring Accountable Officer to immediately submit the liquidation documents and refund, if any, the excess of the cash advance relative to the participation of the municipal officials and employees in the conference for the Executive-Legislative Agenda for CY 2019 to close the cash advance and have the expenditures recorded without delay in the proper expense accounts, thereby enable the Audit Team to conduct timely audit on the propriety and validity of the disbursements in accordance with Section 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997; and
Henceforth, strictly require officials and employees to liquidate cash advances within the prescribed time in accordance with the provisions of the above-stated regulation to forestall misuse and misappropriation of government funds and avoid the appurtenant penalties.

We recommended that Management strictly require their officials and employees to submit detailed and completely accomplished Itinerary of Travel prior to granting of cash advances for official travels pursuant to Section 3.1.1.1 of COA Circular No. 96-004 dated April 19, 1996 and Section 1.1.4.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to ensure that the amounts thereof are not excessive.

We recommended that the Municipality thru its Local School Board incorporate programs, projects and activities that are responsive to ECCD out of the SEF pursuant to Republic Act No. 10410 and to properly identify its revenue sources as required by the Local Government Code in order to present consistent and realistic estimate of available resources in the annual budget of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management submit the ordinance approved by the Sanggunian Bayan, if any, authorizing the grant of allowances to national government officials assigned in the municipality, subject to availability of funds and cease monthly payment thereof without the said ordinance pursuant to Section 447 of Republic Act No. 7160.

We recommended that Management:
Submit in writing a justification on why the subject consultancy services were engaged by the Municipality and why it should not be disallowed as it is tantamount to an unnecessary and irregular expenditure pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012;
Hire consultant/s only for services which require expertise or knowledge that cannot be offered by the present employees of the Municipality to avoid incurrence of unnecessary expenditures; and
Comply with the pertinent provisions of Republic Act No. 9184 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) in the procurement of consultancy services.

We recommended that the LGU pay monetization of leave credits in accordance with the provisions of the Omnibus Rules on Leave ensuring that applications for more than 10 days claim should be paid only up to thirty days in a given year, and claims for 50% of accumulated leaves shall be granted only on valid and justifiable reasons. In addition, the Municipality is enjoined to consider the availability of funds in evaluating the maximum limit to be granted for every monetization application subject to availability of funds, hence, provide equal opportunity among all qualified officials and employees.

We recommended that Management conduct immediately the proceedings for the economic disposal of the unserviceable vehicles of the Municipality, as witnessed by the Audit Team, pursuant to the procedures provided in the Supply and Property Management as prescribed by COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992 to avoid the vehicles’ further deterioration and to generate revenue before the appraisal value grossly devaluates due to the saline environment of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management stop the issuance of travel orders to job order personnel and refrain from paying travelling expenses to said personnel pursuant to Executive Order No. 77. We likewise advise Management to formulate internal guidelines wherein allowable travels of job order workers are streamlined and payments thereof are properly scrutinized as to reasonableness of the purpose, number of travelling personnel and period. In extreme and urgent cases where the services of the J.O personnel are indispensable and travel is extremely necessary considering the limited permanent employees, only the payment of equivalent transportation expenses shall be allowed.
